Loading libraries¶

In [25]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

Loading the dataset¶

In [4]:
# Converting the timestamp column into proper datetime format
call_center_data['Timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(call_center_data['Timestamp'])
call_center_data['Timestamp'].info()

<class 'pandas.core.series.Series'>
RangeIndex: 100000 entries, 0 to 99999
Series name: Timestamp
Non-Null Count   Dtype         
--------------   -----         
100000 non-null  datetime64[ns]
dtypes: datetime64[ns](1)
memory usage: 781.4 KB
In [5]:
# Sort by timestamp to ensure chronological order
call_center_data = call_center_data.sort_values('Timestamp')

# Creating an hourly aggregation of call counts, resample('H') groups data into hourly buckets, size() counts the number of calls per hour
hourly_calls = (call_center_data.set_index('Timestamp').resample('h').size())

# Plot the historical hourly call volume
# This helps visually inspect trends, seasonality, spikes, and missing periods
plt.figure(figsize=(18,6))

plt.plot(hourly_calls)

plt.title('Hourly Call Volume')
plt.xlabel('Time')
plt.ylabel('Number of Calls')

plt.show()

Building the ARIMA model¶

In [22]:
# Spliting the dataset into training and testing sets
train_size = int(len(hourly_calls) * 0.8) # Using 80% for training and 20% for testing

# Using the earlier portion of the series for training
# Time series data must preserve chronological order
train = hourly_calls.iloc[:train_size]

# Using the later portion for testing future predictions
test = hourly_calls.iloc[train_size:]

# ARIMA model order=(p,d,q)
# p = autoregressive terms
# d = differencing order to remove trend/non-stationarity
# q = moving average terms
model = ARIMA(train,order=(2,1,2))

# Fit the model to the training data
model_fit = model.fit()
print(model_fit.summary())

# Forecasting the same number of periods as the test set
# This allows direct comparison against actual observed values
arima_forecast = model_fit.forecast(steps=len(test))

# Calculating the Root Mean Squared Error
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, arima_forecast))

                               SARIMAX Results

Building a SARIMA model¶

In [23]:
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX

# Get exogenous features for each hour
exog = pd.DataFrame(index=hourly_calls.index)
exog['day_of_week'] = exog.index.dayofweek
exog['hour'] = exog.index.hour

# seasonal_order=(P,D,Q,s)
# s=24 means the model assumes patterns repeat every 24 hours, which is appropriate for hourly call center data

sarima_model = SARIMAX(train, order=(1,1,1), seasonal_order=(1,1,1,24))

# Fit the SARIMA model to the training data
sarima_results = sarima_model.fit()
print(sarima_results.summary())

# Forecast future hourly call volume
sarima_forecast = sarima_results.forecast(steps=len(test)) # Forecast horizon matches the size of the test dataset

# Evaluating forecasting accuracy using RMSE
sarima_rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, sarima_forecast))

Plotting the actual vs forecasted values¶

In [19]:
plt.figure(figsize=(18,8))

# Actual values
plt.plot(test.index, test, label='Actual', linewidth=2)

# ARIMA forecast
plt.plot(test.index,  arima_forecast, label='ARIMA Forecast', linewidth=2)

# SARIMA forecast
plt.plot(test.index, sarima_forecast, label='SARIMA Forecast', linewidth=2)

plt.title('Model Comparison: ARIMA vs SARIMA')
plt.xlabel('Time')
plt.ylabel('Call Volume')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

ARIMA Diagnostic plots¶

In [26]:
fig = model_fit.plot_diagnostics(figsize=(12, 8))
plt.tight_layout() # Adjust layout to prevent overlap
plt.show()

SARIMA Diagnostic plots¶

In [11]:
fig = sarima_results.plot_diagnostics(figsize=(12, 8))
plt.tight_layout() # Adjust layout to prevent overlap
plt.show()
In [14]:
metrics = {
    'Model': ['SARIMA', 'ARIMA'],
    'AIC': [sarima_results.aic, model_fit.aic],
    'BIC': [sarima_results.bic, model_fit.bic],
    'RMSE': [
        np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, sarima_forecast)),
        np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, arima_forecast))]}

comparison_df = pd.DataFrame(metrics)
print(comparison_df)

    Model           AIC           BIC      RMSE
0  SARIMA  36885.816233  36920.072402  4.638540
1   ARIMA  36878.318747  36912.592072  4.549053

print("\nRanked by RMSE:")
print(comparison_df.sort_values('RMSE'))

print("\nRanked by AIC:")
print(comparison_df.sort_values('AIC'))
